concept_name: Force on a Charge in an Electric Field (F = qE)
source_book: NCERT Class 12 Physics, Ch.1 Electric Charges and Fields (§1.7 Electric Field, force on a charge) — table-of-contents + Indian-anchor reference only; teaching authored from first principles. HC Verma consulted for SEQUENCE (define the force from E = F/q rearranged → direction set by the sign of q → magnitude F = qE → constant force gives constant acceleration a = qE/m → a sideways launch curves into a parabola, exactly like gravity → mass changes the curvature, not the force), never copied. The atomic idea: a charge q placed in a field E feels a force F = qE — along E for +q, opposite E for −q; because the field between parallel plates is uniform, that force is constant, so the charge moves like a projectile under gravity and a sideways launch traces a parabola. The companion/inverse of electric_field_point_charge (which builds the field; this one feels it). Conceptual-only (no mode_overrides), EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches; misconceptions confronted inside EPIC-L via Rule 16a). field_3d renderer, new uniform_field_force scenario.

real_world_anchor.primary: An inkjet printer fires tiny charged ink droplets between two charged plates. The uniform field between the plates pushes each droplet sideways with a constant force F = qE, curving its path into a parabola so it lands on exactly the right spot on the page. Change the charge on a droplet and you change where it lands — that is how the printer steers ink without any moving nozzle.
real_world_anchor.secondary: An old CRT television steers a beam of electrons the same way. The electrons pass between charged plates, feel a constant sideways force F = qE, and bend into a parabola to hit the right point on the screen — sweeping fast enough to paint the whole picture, line by line.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: An electrostatic precipitator in an Indian coal thermal power plant charges the ash particles in the flue gas, then a strong field pulls each charged speck sideways with F = qE onto a collecting plate — cleaning the smoke before it leaves the chimney. Same law, industrial scale.

formulas.force_definition: F = q E  (rearranged from the field definition E = F / q)
formulas.direction: F is along E for a positive charge, opposite to E for a negative charge
formulas.acceleration: a = F / m = q E / m  (constant, because E is uniform)
formulas.trajectory: a sideways launch traces a parabola — constant force, exactly like projectile motion under gravity

constraints: F = qE depends on the charge and the field only — it does NOT depend on the charge's velocity (unlike the magnetic force qv×B, the electric force acts even on a charge at rest)
Between parallel plates the field is uniform, so the force F = qE is constant in size and direction everywhere in the gap
A constant force gives a constant acceleration a = qE/m — the charge moves like a projectile under gravity
The force F always points along E (for +q); the velocity can point any way, so the force direction and the motion direction are generally different
Doubling the charge or the field doubles the force; a heavier mass gives a smaller acceleration for the same force (a = qE/m)

aha_moment.statement: Constant force F = qE bends a sideways launch into a parabola, like gravity.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: Launch the charge across the field; the green force stays fixed downward while the path bends into a parabola, just like a ball thrown sideways under gravity.

STATE_1.title: A Charge in a Field Feels a Force — F = qE

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is a uniform electric field between two charged plates — every field line points the same way, straight down.
Place a positive charge q in it. The field grabs the charge and pushes it with a force F.
We already know E equals F over q. Rearrange it: the force on the charge is F equals q times E.
So the field is not just a picture — it is what pushes any charge you place inside it.

STATE_2.title: Sign Sets the Direction — Flip q to Negative

tts_sentences.text_en: Before I change anything — predict. If the charge is negative, which way does the force point?
Watch the force arrow. For a negative charge it flips and points UP — opposite to the field.
The size F equals q E is unchanged. Only the direction reversed, because the sign of q reversed.
Positive charge: force along E. Negative charge: force opposite E. The sign owns the direction.

misconception_watch.belief: A negative charge feels no force in a field, or feels a force in the same direction as a positive charge
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Flip q negative and watch the force arrow reverse to point up, opposite the field — same size, opposite direction.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: F = qE: the force is along E for +q and opposite E for −q; the magnitude qE is the same.

STATE_3.title: How Big? — F = qE Scales with q and E

tts_sentences.text_en: How big is the push? The force is F equals q times E — nothing else.
Double the charge and the force doubles. The field has twice as much charge to pull on.
Move to a stronger field — denser field lines — and the same charge feels a bigger force.
There is no velocity in this formula. A charge sitting perfectly still still feels the full force qE.

STATE_4.title: Constant Force, Constant Acceleration — a = qE/m

tts_sentences.text_en: Now let the charge go from rest. The force is constant, so the charge accelerates straight along the field.
Newton's law gives the acceleration: a equals F over m, which is q E over m.
The force arrow stays the same length the whole way — but the velocity keeps growing.
This is exactly free fall, but with the electric force qE playing the role of gravity.

STATE_5.title: Launch It Sideways — the Path Is a Parabola

tts_sentences.text_en: Now launch the charge sideways across the field. Predict — what shape will its path be?
Watch. The force stays fixed, pointing along the field, while the path bends into a parabola.
The force never points where the charge is going. It is sideways to the motion, yet it curves the path.
This is exactly a ball thrown sideways under gravity. A constant force always gives a parabola.

misconception_watch.belief: The force pushes the charge along its direction of motion, so the charge speeds straight down the field line it sits on
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Launch the charge sideways; the green force stays fixed downward while the velocity points along the curving path — force and motion are clearly different directions.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: F = qE sets the force direction (along E), not the motion direction; a constant sideways force bends the path into a parabola.

STATE_6.title: Same Force, Different Mass — a = qE/m

tts_sentences.text_en: Send in two charges with the same charge and the same field — but different masses.
They feel the exact same force F equals q E. But watch their paths split apart.
The light one whips into a sharp curve. The heavy one barely bends. Same force, different acceleration.
Because a equals q E over m: a bigger mass gives a smaller acceleration for the very same force.

misconception_watch.belief: A bigger charge or a stronger field always makes the charge move faster, regardless of its mass
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Two charges with identical q and E but different mass curve by different amounts — the heavier one accelerates less.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: The force is qE, but the motion is a = qE/m — mass matters: heavier means less acceleration for the same force.

STATE_7.title: Explore — Drive q, Sign, Field and Mass

tts_sentences.text_en: Now you drive it. Change the charge, flip its sign, change the field strength and the mass.
Watch the force F equals q E grow and flip, and watch the parabola tighten or stretch.
A bigger charge or field steepens the curve; a bigger mass flattens it — that is a equals q E over m.
One law runs all of it: the force is q E, and the motion is q E divided by the mass.

STATE_1.label: A charge in a field feels a force — F = qE

STATE_3.label: How big — F = qE scales with q and E

STATE_6.label: Same force, different mass — a = qE/m

STATE_7.label: Free explore — q, sign, field, mass
